title: Complicated Mania
abstract:
Complicated Mania
Comorbidity and Immediate Outcome in the Treatment of Mania
Donald W. Black, MD; George Winokur, MD; Sue Bell, MA; Amelia Nasrallah, MA; James Hulbert, PhD
\s=b\In a case-control study, 57 manics with antecedent or
coexisting nonaffective psychiatric disorders (n = 38) or seri-
ous medical illnesses (n = 19) ("complicated mania") were
compared with 114 age-, sex-, and year-of-admisson\p=m-\matched
controls with no other disorder ("uncomplicated mania").
Significant differences emerged between the three groups in
age, marital status, age at onset, number of prior hospitaliza-
tions and prior suicide attempts, organic features, and out-
come measures (recovery and death rates). Patients were
divided into four treatment groups based on primary mode of
therapy during index admission; the groups included electro-
convulsive therapy, adequate lithium carbonate, inadequate
lithium carbonate, and neither treatment. Uncomplicated ma-
nics were significantly more likely to receive adequate lithium
carbonate and less likely to receive inadequate lithium car-
bonate than were complicated manics. The latter patients had
a significantly poorer immediate response to treatment overall,
and to adequate lithium carbonate specifically. Seventy-eight
(68.4%) uncomplicated manics had recovered at discharge,
compared with 26 (45.6%) complicated manics. Logistic re-
gression suggested that the influence of comorbidity on
outcome was more important for women than men. We con-
clude that complicated mania is a useful clinical construct.
(Arch Gen Psychiatry 1988;45:232-236)
Comorbidity,
or the concurrence of two or more disor¬
ders, has generated increasing attention. The concept
has been incorporated into diagnosis related groups and
influences hospital reimbursement.1 More importantly,
psychiatric patients with comorbidity differ in substantial
ways from those without2*4 and comorbidity is demon¬
strated to affect the length of hospital stay,5 response to
somatic treatment,2*4 and mortality.6
The concept of comorbidity in psychiatric disorders
derives from efforts to separate primary from secondary
affective illness. This effort originated in Britain in the
1960s7 and was subsequently elaborated by investigators
at Washington University, St Louis. Briefly, Feighner et
al8 defined secondary depression as occurring after the
onset of a nonaffective psychiatric disorder or serious
medical illness. Patients with serious medical illnesses
were later deleted from the definition. The modified con¬
cept was then incorporated into the Research Diagnostic
Criteria.9 The concept of a secondary illness was never
extended to manic disorders because of the widespread
belief that bipolar disorders are primary and rarely, if ever,
secondary.10 This belief was first questioned by Krautham¬
mer and Klerman,11 who reviewed the literature and re¬
ported 21 cases of manic syndromes ("secondary mania")
following or in association with medical or pharmacologie
conditions. They found secondary manies to have an older
age at onset than primary manies and to lack a family
history of affective disorder. Since their review, many cases
of manic syndromes secondary to physical or pharmacologie
causes
have been reported.
In
an exhaustive review,
Cummings12 identified 25 neurologic, toxic, or metabolic
disorders and 18 types of drugs associated with secondary
mania. Shukla and coworkers13 looked at manic syndromes
following head injury in 20 patients. They reported that
patients with a history of closed head trauma were likely
to have irritable mood and no family history of bipolar
disorders. Like Krauthammer and Klerman,11 these au¬
thors all imply a causal relationship between a physical or
pharmacologie factor and a subsequent manic syndrome,
which, therefore, might best be termed an induced mania,
but which nonetheless differs in many respects from
primary mania. On the other hand, the term secondary as
used in describing depressive illness, has been meant to
imply chronology, not etiology.
DSM-III14 and its recent revision15 do not incorporate
the primary/secondary paradigm for major depression.
However, they provide Axis II and Axis III for coding
coexisting personality and physical disorders, respectively.
The multiaxial system is meant to ensure that the presence
of conditions other than the major psychiatric syndrome
are not ignored,
so that all relevant information will be
used in the clinical formulation. This concept has proved
useful. For example, work by Pfohl et al2 has demonstrated
that patients with major depression and
a concurrent
personality disorder differ substantially from patients
suffering from depression alone. They demonstrate that
patients with concurrent personality disorder differ clini¬
cally, have a different response to dexamethasone, and are
more likely to have family members with antisocial person¬
ality or alcoholism. They also tend to respond poorly to
pharmacotherapy. Other researchers in personality disor¬
der have also found differences in clinical features and
response
to
treatment.1619 Likewise,
Rounsaville
and
coworkers3 have demonstrated that additional DSM-III
diagnoses in alcoholics generally predict poorer treatment
outcome at a one-year follow-up examination.
The literature on secondary depression also suggests
that subjects two disorders differ from others only one.20*21
Clayton and Lewis20 reviewed the literature and noted that
although the symptom pictures of primary and secondary
depressions are similar, there are substantial differences
in age, sex, neurophysiologic variables, and family history.
Furthermore, there is an emerging consensus that second¬
ary depressives have a poor response to both electrocon-
vulsive therapy
(ECT)22*26 and antidepressant medica¬
tion,26*27 although not all reports are in agreement.28 Thus,
comorbidity seems to have an impact on treatment outcome
in a variety of psychiatric disorders.
In a recent report on secondary depression, Winokur et
Accepted for publication Oct 16, 1987.
From the Department of Psychiatry, University of Iowa College of
Medicine, Iowa City.
Reprint requests to Psychiatric Hospital, 500 Newton Rd, Iowa City, IA
52242 (Dr Black).

 al4 compared subjects who had a serious concurrent medical
illness with subjects who had an antecedent or coexisting
psychiatric disorder such as alcoholism or somatization
disorder. They demonstrated that patients with concurrent
nonaffective
psychiatric
disorders
("neurotics")
were
younger, more likely to have engaged in a previous suicide
attempt, and less likely to have memory impairment on the
mental status examination than patients with medical
illnesses. In follow-up, these subjects were less likely to
show improvement and were more likely to relapse early
in follow-up. Thus, depressed patients with other nonaffec¬
tive psychiatric disorders were posited to fit the concept
of "neurotic" depression and those with depression and a
concurrent medical illness to fit the concept of a "reactive"
depression. These findings led us to believe that a similar
breakdown might be useful in manic disorders. We hypoth¬
esized that manic disorders occurring in the context of
another nonaffective psychiatric disorder or
a serious

physical illness would differ substantially from those with¬
out these conditions in terms of clinical and demographic
variables, treatment received, response to treatment, and
outcome
measures.
We further hypothesized that any
differences that emerged might offer insight into the
etiology of manic disorders and their treatment.
We have chosen not to use the term secondary mania,
which is
now associated with the Krauthammer and
Klerman11 concept of a mania resulting from a physical or
pharmacologie insult.
An alternative definition would
merely create confusion. Therefore,
we
use the term
complicated mania to designate manic syndromes occur¬
ring in the context of antecedent or coexistent psychiatric
disorders or a concurrent serious medical disorder. This
may have advantages over a primary/secondary paradigm
in that it is often difficult to determine whether the
affective syndrome or complicating illness came first. This
problem can be avoided by considering such patients as
either complicated or uncomplicated. Our approach is also
compatible with the multiaxial diagnostic system in DSM-
III in which all relevant mental and physical conditions are
acknowledged, but no chronologic order is assigned.
METHODS
The 438 manies satisfying Research Diagnostic Criteria9 for
either mania (n = 396) or schizoaffective disorder, manic type
(schizomania) (n = 42), who were included in the study had been
hospitalized at the University of Iowa Psychiatric Hospital, Iowa
City, between Jan 1,
1970,
and Dec 31,
1981.
We included
schizomanic patients because their clinical features, family history,
and treatment response are reported to be similar to those of
patients with bipolar affective disorder, manic type.29·30
Patients were considered to have a complicated mania if their
index manic episode was preceded or accompanied by a nonaffec¬
tive DSM-III psychiatric disorder or by a serious or potentially
life-threatening medical illness, including cancer or chronic ob-

structive lung disease but not well-controlled hypertension or
dysfunctional uterine bleeding. Fifty-seven manies were consid¬
ered "complicated" and their specific diagnoses are presented in
(medical or psychiatric), while 15 (26.3%) had two and one (1.8%)
had three. Each complicated manic was then randomly matched
by age, sex, and year of admission with two "uncomplicated"
controls who had mania but no other disorder. The charts were
carefully reviewed by one of us (A.N.), a masters-level psycholo¬
gist, for demographic, clinical treatment, and outcome data. These
patients make up a subset of manic patients described elsewhere.31
Global assessment of the patient by the physicians in the final
progress notes and discharge summary was used to rate outcome.
Five ratings were created: recovered, if the note suggested that
the patient was entirely well at discharge and had no manic
symptoms; improved, for those reported as having a good response
to treatment but who may have had one or more intermittent signs
of mania at discharge; partially improved, for those judged by
physicians as being only mildly or modestly improved, but still
exhibiting one or more persistent manic symptoms at discharge;
unimproved or worse; and AMA, for those discharging themselves
For comparison, the patients were divided into four treatment
groups depending on the primary mode of therapy during index
hospitalization: (1) patients who received ECT during the hospi¬
talization (n = 16); (2) patients who received adequate lithium
carbonate treatment ( = 77); (3) patients who received inadequate
lithium carbonate (n = 62); and (4) patients who received neither
ECT nor lithium carbonate (n=16). Patients treated by ECT
received, on average, 8.4 modified treatments using either sinusoi¬
dal wave or brief pulse stimulation. Adequate lithium carbonate
a minimum of two weeks of lithium
carbonate treatment with a lithium blood level of at least 0.9
mmol/L (0.9 mEq/L) for one week. The lithium blood level was
recorded by noting the highest level achieved in the chart and
sustained for at least one week within a range of ±0.3 mmol/L
(±0.3 mEq/L). All patients receiving lithium carbonate but not
meeting these requirements were assigned to the inadequate
lithium treatment group. Patients not receiving ECT or lithium
carbonate may have received other treatments that were either
somatic or psychosocial. A majority of patients in all groups
received antipsychotics but the amount was not quantified.
A severity score was assigned to all patients based on the
presence/absence of eight factors deemed by one of us (G.W.) to
indicate symptomatic severity on admission, including early onset,
acute course, hallucinations, delusions, suicide thoughts, organic
features, lack of precipitating event, and prior hospitalizations.
Deaths were determined by electronically matching the list of
171 manies against Iowa death certificates for the period from Jan
1, 1970, through Dec 31, 1983. During follow-up 11 (6.4%) patients
died (four uncomplicated, seven complicated).
Categorical variables were compared with the  2 statistic.
Continuous variables were compared by analysis of variance.
Logistic regression was performed using programs from the
Statistical Analysis System (SAS). Recovery (0 = poor, 1 = good)
was entered into the regression model as the dependent variable.
Table 2 shows the clinical and demographic differences between
uncomplicated manies and two groups of complicated manies—
"medical" and "psychiatric." Complicated manies in the medical
cluster had medical or organic mental disorders, while manies in
the psychiatric cluster had antecedent or coexistent nonaffective
psychiatric disorders, as outlined in Table 1. Four patients had a
combination of psychiatric and physical disorders and were in¬
cluded in the medical cluster for the analysis. A three-way
comparison between uncomplicated manies and the two compii-
cated clusters shows significant differences in age, marital status,
number of prior psychiatric hospitalizations, age at onset, prior
suicide attempts, organic features (such as disorientation and
memory loss), and outcome (recovery and death rates). Medical
cluster patients were older, had an older age at onset, had more
prior psychiatric hospitalizations, had more organic features, had
no suicide attempts, had poor recovery rates, and had high death
rates. Psychiatric cluster patients were likely to be male, be
divorced or separated, have few prior psychiatric hospitalizations,
have an early age at onset, have prior suicide attempts, and have
poor recovery rates. There was essentially no difference in the
recovery rates for the medical and psychiatric cluster patients
(50.0% vs 43.6%). Although the divorce rates did not differ between
psychiatric and medical cluster patients, the much older age of
the latter group suggests that the rate in psychiatric cluster
patients is higher than what would be expected; this is what we
find when the rate is compared with that among controls (30.8%
vs 13.8%, a significant difference:  2
Table 3 compares the percentage of patients distributed in the
four treatment groups. Uncomplicated manies were significantly
more likely to have received adequate lithium carbonate therapy,
less likely to have received inadequate lithium carbonate therapy,
more likely to have received adequate treatment (ie, either ECT
or adequate lithium carbonate), and less likely to have received
inadequate therapy (ie, inadequate lithium carbonate or neither
treatment). Seventy-four (64.9%) uncomplicated manies received
adequate treatment, compared with only 19 (33.4%) complicated
Patients receiving lithium carbonate in adequate amounts were
significantly more likely to achieve recovery if their illness was
uncomplicated (Table 4). Combining all treatment groups, 78
(68.4%) uncomplicated manies had recovered at hospital discharge,
but only 26 (45.6%) complicated manies had, a highly significant
difference ( 2 = 8.30,  = .004). Among uncomplicated manies, the
groups receiving ECT, adequate lithium carbonate, and inadequate
lithium carbonate each had significantly better outcome than those
receiving neither treatment. There were no significant differences
in outcome among complicated manies within the four treatment
groups. Of manies receiving adequate therapy (ie, either ECT or
adequate lithium carbonate), uncomplicated patients had signifi¬
cantly higher recovery rates than complicated patients (74.3% vs
42.1%;  2 = 7.18; P<.007). Among inadequately treated patients
there was a nonsignificant trend for more uncomplicated manies
to have recovered than complicated manies.
Twelve selected demographic, clinical, and treatment variables
were entered into a logistic regression model and odds ratios were
calculated. Overall, only comorbidity (the presence of a compli¬
cated disorder) was significantly associated with poor outcome
(Table 5). We then repeated the analysis for men and women
separately and found that the association between comorbidity
and poor outcome was significant for women but not for men.
Complicated mania appears to be a useful clinical con¬
struct. Among our findings, we determined that compli¬
cated manies differ clinically and demographically from
uncomplicated manies, receive inadequate somatic therapy,
and respond poorly to somatic treatment at hospital dis¬
charge. Complicated manies within medical and psychiatric
clusters also differed. Psychiatric cluster patients tended
to have fewer prior psychiatric hospitalizations, have early
onset of illness, and have prior suicide attempts, while
medical cluster patients had a later age at onset, no prior
suicide attempts, more organic features (ie, disorientation
and memory loss), and more deaths during follow-up. While
many of the differences between these two groups are
probably due to age, such as number of prior hospitaliza¬
tions and high death rates, many, such as prior suicide
attempts, are probably not. The psychiatric cluster patients
in many ways resemble the "neurotic" secondary depres¬
sives described by Winokur et al,4 who displayed early
onset, suicidal behavior, and few organic features, and had
poor treatment response, all occurring in the context of
stormy life events. Similarities between psychiatric cluster
manies and neurotic secondary depressives may reflect, in
part, comorbidity from a nonaffective psychiatric disorder.
For example, antisocial personality and alcoholism both
predispose to suicidal attempts, marital discord, and other
problems in living.32*35 Thus, while bipolar illness is clearly
more biologically "weighted" than depression, both appear
to be modified by psychiatric comorbidity.
The medical cluster patients, on the other hand, seem to
fit the "induced" mania model suggested by Krauthammer
and Klerman11 by virtue of older age, older age at onset,
significant organic features, poor treatment response, and
high death rate. These patients also seem to resemble the
medical secondary depressives described by Winokur et
al4 and fit their model of a "reactive" illness in that they
occur late and in the context of fewer psychosocial difficul¬
ties. Although it is unlikely that the mania occurring in
the medically ill patient is "reactive" in the sense that it
a psychosocial precipitant, it is
possible that it occurred in response to, or was induced by,
medical or neurologic factors such as metastatic cancer or
a delirium. It is clear that we can divide manies into those
or without complications, and among those with
complications there are two distinct and separable groups.
Complicated manies had poor immediate outcome com¬
pared with uncomplicated manies. We found two explana¬
tions for this. First, complicated manies tended to be
inadequately treated compared with uncomplicated manies
(64.9% vs 33.4%, respectively). Reasons for this under-
treatment are complex, but it may be that complicated
manies are less cooperative in taking medication, refuse
medication, or demand discharge against medical advice.
In fact, six (10.5%) complicated manies left against medical
advice compared with four (3.5%) uncomplicated manies
( 2 = 3.40;  = .065). Possibly the irritability and uncoope-
rativeness typical of mania, exacerbated by substance
an underlying personality disorder,
disturbance such as delirium, precludes adequate compli¬
ance. Alternatively, ward physicians may have viewed
complicated manies as having atypical illnesses and believed
that these patients either did not warrant medication or
would not respond to medication and so tended to treat them
less aggressively. Also, 18(31.6%)patients with complicated
mania had serious medical or organic mental disorders so
that ward physicians may simply have been reluctant to use
medication or ECT. (No medically ill manic in our series
received ECT.) It is not likely that the undertreatment was
due to a shorter length of stay, however, in that the three
groups had comparable hospital stays.
Apart from undertreatment, the second reason for poor
immediate outcome is that complicated manies failed to
respond to treatment as well as uncomplicated manies.
This was true for the group receiving adequate lithium
carbonate treatment, the group receiving "adequate ther¬
apy" (ECT and adequate lithium carbonate combined), and
overall (by combining the four treatment groups). There
were no significant differences in recovery rates among
patients receiving inadequate treatment (ie, either inade¬
quate lithium carbonate or neither treatment), nor were
there any significant differences in outcome among the
four treatment groups within the group of complicated
manies. Of course, lower serum lithium levels (ie, 0.35 to
0.75 mmol/L [0.35 to 0.70 mEq/L]) may represent appro¬
priate therapy for the older medically ill manic. The serum
levels required by our definition of "adequate treatment"
may prove toxic to these persons. By way of comparison,
among the medically ill, patients receiving adequate lith¬
ium carbonate fared about the same as those who received
inadequate lithium carbonate (two [33.3%] vs four [44.4%]
recovered, respectively). The numbers suggest that even
in different amounts, lithium carbonate may not be
effective in the medically ill manic.
The association of comorbidity with poor immediate
response was also confirmed by logistic regression. Using
the odds ratio, a manic with comorbidity would have less
a 50% chance of having recovered at discharge,
compared with one without. The findings further suggest
that women with comorbidity (odds ratio = 0.19) are less
likely to recover than men (odds ratio = 0.52). Naturally,
these findings must be considered tentative, but they may
have important treatment implications.
It is clear from the data that adequate treatment is
associated with recovery in patients with uncomplicated
mania. It is less clear that adequate treatment has much
influence on outcome in complicated mania. Complicated
manies receiving adequate lithium carbonate actually had
worse outcomes than manies in the three other treatment
groups, although these differences were not significant.
Possibly these patients do not respond well to treatment
for constitutional (ie, biologic/genetic) reasons, or are rated
by clinicians as poor responders because they complain
more about side effects (or develop more side effects). If it
is true that complicated manies do not respond well to
currently available biologic therapies, then investigators
will need to develop new treatment strategies, pharmaco¬
logie and/or psychosocial, to treat these patients satisfac¬
torily. Himmelhoch36 and others37*38 have proposed that
carbamazepine may be a suitable alternative to lithium
carbonate in atypical mania. It would be interesting to see
the effect of carbamazepine in our complicated manies, as
many might possibly be considered atypical by these
investigators. Unfortunately, we were unable to look at
this, as carbamazepine was rarely used for the treatment
of mania during the study years 1970 through 1987.
Our study was based on a chart review rather than
interviews with subjects. This approach may tend to
underdiagnose complicating medical and psychiatric dis¬
orders; in fact, in our study, only 57 (13%) of 438 manies
complicated mania. Ward psychiatrists may have focused
on the manic syndrome to the exclusion of other disorders.
Next, a naturalistic study is not a substitute for a controlled
prospective design, although results of naturalistic and
are often similar.39 Also, the four
treatment groups are meant to approximate four general
treatment schemes, but clearly cannot take into account
the subtleties of individualized treatment, especially with
those patients who are difficult to treat. Finally, the sample
of complicated manies (57) was small, limiting our ability
to detect differences between treatment groups.
